md: If someone shows you the equation $y = \sin\theta$ and tells you to memorize it, you've already lost. **Sine is not a formula — it's a measurement.** Specifically: the **height** of a point that's going around a circle at constant speed.
Here's a Ferris wheel. Press Play and watch the highlighted rider.

The rider is going in a circle. But if you focus only on how *high* they are above the wheel's center, that height rises and falls — smoothly, repeatedly, between $+1$ and $-1$ (in units of the wheel's radius). The graph on the right traces that height over time. **It's a sine wave. There's no formula being applied — that's literally just the rider's altitude.**
Switch the display to `x-pos (cos)` to see what the *horizontal* position does instead. Same circular motion, different measurement: now you get a cosine wave. Same shape as sine but shifted by 90°.

blocks.title: The takeaway
blocks.body: A point rotating uniformly around a circle produces TWO simultaneous oscillations: its height (= sine) and its horizontal position (= cosine). The two functions are tied together because they're the two coordinates of the same rotating thing. That is why $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ — it's the Pythagorean theorem applied to those two coordinates.

md: Anything that goes around in a circle — or anything that *behaves like* it goes around in a circle — produces sine and cosine waves as side-effects:
**Sound:** vibrating eardrum, swinging pendulum, plucked guitar string → all sinusoidal.
**Light & radio:** AC current, electromagnetic waves → sinusoidal.
**Tides:** moon orbiting Earth → sinusoidal tidal cycle.
**Mechanical engines:** pistons driven by a rotating crankshaft → their height vs. time is sine.
Trig isn't really about triangles. It's about **rotations**, and the linear shadows rotations cast.
Now the textbook definitions make sense:
$\sin\theta = y\text{-coordinate of the unit-circle point at angle }\theta$
$\cos\theta = x\text{-coordinate of the unit-circle point at angle }\theta$

These aren't arbitrary abbreviations — they label the two measurements you can extract from a rotating point. The wave shapes are just what those measurements look like, plotted against time (or angle).
Watch both at once. The red curve (sine) starts at 0 and goes up. The green curve (cosine) starts at 1 and goes down. They're partners — always exactly 90° apart, always with $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$.

blocks.title: Try this
blocks.body: Crank up the speed slider. The wave gets compressed horizontally — same shape, more cycles per second. That's **frequency**. A faster Ferris wheel = a higher-pitched sine wave. This is exactly how audio frequencies work — higher pitch = faster rotation.
